transcript:
Right now you're watching 600 points explode outward and then, within seconds, give up. What starts as a firework collapses into a small trembling knot of embers, permanently.

Here's the thing worth noticing: nothing in this code ever adds energy back. Look at the array name itself — it's called "what will settle," not "particles." Every particle was doomed to its final position the moment it was born. And the color logic, in the function called "velocity to hue," is really a thermometer: fast-moving particles glow cyan, the "brightness of not-yet-lost" as the comment says, cooling through amber into a rust color the artist literally labeled "rustSediment... the floor color: what remains."

The gravity target — the "attractor" — physically sinks toward a floor line over the course of the piece. That's not decoration. It's forcing everything downward while friction, named "velocityBleed," quietly drains motion every single frame, no exceptions, no refills.

So what you're looking at isn't a firework dying. It's a system being asked: what's left of you when nothing is added and nothing is spared? The honeycomb core is the answer — the sediment, not the performance.
